humanStrength: Exception handling, mixed inventory, damaged items, judgment calls
summary: Structured warehouses are one of the clearest early markets for humanoid and mobile manipulation robots because tasks repeat, routes can be mapped, and safety zones can be controlled.

humanStrength: Root-cause judgment, safety escalation, repair decisions
summary: Inspection is already practical when robots patrol known environments, collect repeatable sensor data, and hand off decisions to human operators.

humanStrength: Fixture changes, quality nuance, unexpected variation
summary: Repetitive handling with defined parts remains one of the strongest robot advantage zones, especially when vision models and fixtures reduce variation.

humanStrength: Trust, empathy, relationship depth, social context
summary: Companion robots are less about replacing people and more about persistent presence, behavioral cues, and support for loneliness or routine.

humanStrength: Care judgment, dignity, emotional nuance, medical escalation
summary: Robots may help with reminders, presence, remote check-ins, and basic monitoring, while humans remain central for care, trust, and medical decisions.
Surgery shows how robots can outperform humans in precision while still depending on human surgeons for decisions, accountability, and patient context.

humanStrength: Dense urban judgment, customer interaction, edge-case navigation
summary: Delivery robots work best in constrained campuses, sidewalks, and known service zones where the environment can be monitored and optimized.

humanStrength: Trust, ethics, emotional labor, unpredictable human needs
summary: Robots can assist with reminders and monitoring, but deep caregiving remains a human-led domain because the work is relational, ethical, and highly contextual.

humanStrength: Taste, responsibility, values, social impact, narrative judgment
summary: AI can support research and drafting, but decisions involving taste, responsibility, and social consequences remain deeply human.
